common_name: Boniato (White Sweet Potato, Cuban Sweet Potato, Batata)

planting.note: Plant slips (rooted cuttings) after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm above 65°F; boniato slips root readily; plant in well-mounded ridges for best tuber development and drainage.

directions: Plant rooted slips in well-drained, sandy-loam, enriched ridges in full sun after last frost when soil is warm; boniato is the traditional sweet potato of Cuban, Caribbean, and Latin American cuisine, prized for its dry, fluffy, white to pale cream flesh that is less sweet, less moist, and more starchy than orange sweet potatoes — more similar in texture to a russet potato and much preferred in savory preparations. The skin is irregularly shaped, pinkish to light brown, and the plants are slightly more drought-tolerant than standard sweet potatoes. Excellent roasted, mashed, or fried; does not have the cloying sweetness that makes American orange sweet potatoes unsuitable for some savory applications. Stores well in a cool, dry location.

common_name: Okinawan Sweet Potato (Purple Sweet Potato, Hawaiian Purple Yam)

planting.note: Plant slips in warm soil (above 65°F) after last frost; Okinawan purple sweet potato requires a longer growing season (100–130 days) than standard varieties — plant as early as possible in the season in the South.

directions: Plant rooted slips in well-drained, loose, warm soil in full sun after last frost; Okinawan sweet potato has creamy-white skin concealing spectacularly vivid purple-violet flesh — the deep purple color comes from extremely high anthocyanin content (among the highest of any sweet potato), making it a genuine superfood with powerful antioxidant properties. The flavor is mildly sweet, slightly nutty, and drier than orange-flesh types; it is prized in Hawaiian cuisine (where it's called 'Hawaiian purple sweet potato'), Japanese cuisine, and modern farm-to-table cooking for its dramatic color that holds remarkably well through most cooking methods. Excellent roasted, steamed, in ice cream, and in colorful desserts.

planting.note: Plant slips in warm soil after last frost; Vardaman is an exceptionally compact sweet potato whose naturally short vines (24–30 inches) make it the best choice for small beds, containers, and home gardens with limited space.

directions: Plant rooted slips in well-drained, sandy-loam soil in full sun after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; Vardaman is a compact, short-vined sweet potato (24–30 inch vines versus 6–10 feet for standard varieties) developed at Mississippi State University specifically for the home garden, where its restrained spread and ornamental purple foliage make it an attractive edible landscape plant. The orange-flesh tubers are typically smaller than commercial varieties but have excellent rich, sweet flavor and moist texture. An outstanding choice for container growing, raised beds, and small garden spaces where standard sweet potatoes would overwhelm their neighbors. Keeps well in storage and is the most popular sweet potato for the home garden in the southeastern United States.

planting.note: Plant certified disease-free slips 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm above 65°F; Beauregard was developed at Louisiana State University AgCenter and is the standard commercial sweet potato of the Gulf South — plant in raised ridges of loose, sandy-loam soil enriched with compost for best tuber development and easy harvest.

directions: Plant certified slips 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy-loam soil built up into ridges in full sun; Beauregard is the most widely grown sweet potato in the United States, developed by the LSU AgCenter in 1987 and released commercially in 1992, producing large, uniform, reddish-purple-skinned tubers with deep orange, moist, sweet flesh of outstanding quality — the standard against which commercial and home-grown sweet potatoes are judged for appearance, yield, and eating quality. It produces consistently large, well-formed tubers even in imperfect soil conditions, has excellent resistance to the diseases that devastate older varieties, and stores exceptionally well. Harvest when vines begin to yellow, typically 90–100 days after planting slips, by carefully digging with a fork to avoid cutting tubers; cure at 85–90°F and 90% humidity for 10–14 days to convert starches to sugars and heal any cuts before long-term storage.

common_name: Murasaki Sweet Potato (Japanese Purple Sweet Potato)

planting.note: Plant slips 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; Murasaki requires a full 95–110 days, so plant as early as possible in the South. It performs well in the Gulf Coast heat and produces excellent tubers with a slightly longer season than standard varieties.

directions: Plant rooted slips 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, loose, sandy-loam soil in full sun; murasaki (purple in Japanese) sweet potato is a Japanese-origin variety producing long, slender tubers with striking dark purple skin and off-white to cream-colored flesh — a distinct and useful contrast to the Okinawan purple-flesh type, with a dry, chestnut-like sweetness and firm texture that holds up beautifully in roasting, frying, and sautéing where orange-flesh sweet potatoes would become too soft. The flavor is rich, mildly sweet, and nutty with a savory quality that makes it more versatile in non-sweet preparations than moist orange types. Widely grown in Japan and increasingly popular in the United States as interest in Asian sweet potato varieties grows; excellent for Japanese-style roasted sweet potatoes, tempura, and savory preparations that benefit from a firmer texture.

planting.note: Plant slips after last frost when soil is warm above 65°F; Georgia Jet is one of the most early-maturing and productive sweet potato varieties for northern and transition-zone growers, reaching harvest in 90 days — useful in zone 9 as a reliable second crop that fits in a shortened growing window. Excellent for home slips from purchased roots.

directions: Plant rooted slips 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, loose, sandy-loam soil in full sun; Georgia Jet is a productive open-pollinated sweet potato variety that reaches harvest in 90 days — one of the earliest-maturing sweet potatoes available, making it a reliable choice for shorter growing seasons and for gardeners in zones 5–7 who need a variety that matures before fall frost. In zone 9 it is equally useful as an early-maturing variety that can be planted as a second crop after spring vegetables finish. The deep reddish-purple skin and vivid orange flesh are very attractive; the flavor is sweet, moist, and rich — considered excellent for baking, steaming, and as a dessert sweet potato. An outstanding heirloom variety that produces well without synthetic fertilizers and stores reliably.

planting.note: Plant certified disease-free slips 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; Covington was developed at North Carolina State University to succeed Beauregard as the leading commercial variety of the Southeast, with better disease resistance and comparable eating quality. It is the most widely grown sweet potato variety in the Mid-Atlantic and Southeast as of 2010.

directions: Plant rooted slips 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy-loam soil built up into ridges in full sun; Covington was released in 2005 by North Carolina State University to replace Beauregard as the standard commercial sweet potato of the mid-Atlantic and Southeast, producing large, uniform, rose-copper-skinned tubers with deep orange, moist, sweet flesh and improved resistance to soil diseases compared to Beauregard — particularly Rhizopus soft rot, which causes significant postharvest losses in warm, humid storage. The eating quality is comparable to or slightly superior to Beauregard, with consistently moist, flavorful flesh and good marketability. It became the dominant sweet potato variety in North Carolina (the leading sweet potato state) within 5 years of release. Outstanding for the Gulf Coast and Southeast where disease-resistant varieties improve dependability through humid summers.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Sweetpotato
North Carolina Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services

common_name: Jewel Sweet Potato (1970 NCSU Orange Standard)

planting.note: Plant certified slips after last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F; Jewel was released in 1970 by North Carolina State University and was the dominant American commercial sweet potato variety from the late 1970s through the 1990s, grown primarily in North Carolina, Louisiana, and Virginia — it remains a reliable home garden standard with excellent moist orange flesh and consistent yields.

directions: Plant slips 12–18 inches apart in loose, well-drained soil in full sun after soil warms above 65°F; Jewel tolerates less-than-ideal soil conditions better than many newer varieties. Jewel was developed and released in 1970 by North Carolina State University as part of the USDA–state cooperative sweet potato breeding program and became the dominant American commercial sweet potato throughout the 1970s–1990s; it produced the standard against which American sweet potatoes were judged for two decades before Beauregard's introduction. The medium to large, elongated, smooth, coppery-orange to red-orange-skinned roots have intensely orange, moist, sweet flesh that softens to a creamy consistency when baked — an excellent texture for traditional sweet potato preparations. Flavor is sweet and complex with good caramel development during baking. Self-reliant and productive; excellent for Gulf Coast spring planting in late March through April and fall planting in June through July.

planting.note: Plant certified slips after last frost when soil is warm above 65°F; O'Henry is a natural sport (mutation) of Beauregard selected for white flesh rather than orange, retaining Beauregard's productive characteristics while producing cream-to-white-fleshed roots favored in Asian cooking traditions that prefer the milder, drier texture of white sweet potatoes.

directions: Plant slips 12–18 inches apart in loose, well-drained soil after soil warms above 65°F; culture is identical to Beauregard. O'Henry sweet potato is a natural sport of Beauregard — a genetic mutation selected from a commercial Beauregard planting — that produces cream to white flesh rather than the typical orange, with tan to buff skin; it retains the vigor, yield, and disease resistance of its Beauregard parent. The white flesh has a distinctly different flavor: less sweet, with a drier, more starchy texture and mild, subtle sweetness rather than the intense caramel-sweetness of orange varieties. This profile is much preferred in Japanese and Korean cooking, where it resembles the traditional white-flesh sweet potatoes of East Asian agriculture and is sold in Asian grocery markets across the US. Excellent roasted whole, in savory preparations, tempura, and for any use where a less-sweet, more neutral-flavored sweet potato is desired.

common_name: Stokes Purple Sweet Potato (Deep Purple Ultra-High Anthocyanin)

planting.note: Plant slips after last frost when soil warms above 65°F; Stokes Purple needs a full 110–125 days and benefits from the long season of Gulf Coast and warm Southern gardens; developed by Stokes Tropicals nursery in Lafayette, Louisiana, selected for extraordinarily high anthocyanin content — higher per serving than blueberries — making it one of the most antioxidant-rich foods commercially available.

directions: Plant slips 12–18 inches apart in loose, well-drained soil after soil warms above 65°F; Stokes Purple needs its full 110–125 days to develop; harvest after first frost threat in cooler climates. Stokes Purple was developed by Stokes Tropicals nursery in Lafayette, Louisiana from crosses involving Asian purple-flesh sweet potato types and selected specifically for American growing conditions and maximum anthocyanin content; the resulting variety has skin ranging from deep purple to nearly black and flesh that is uniformly rich purple-violet throughout — the highest level of anthocyanin pigments of any readily available sweet potato cultivar, higher per serving than blueberries or Concord grapes. The flavor is less sweet and more starchy than orange-flesh types, with an earthy, wine-like complexity and dense texture suited to roasting, savory preparations, smoothies, and applications where the dramatic color matters most. The vivid purple holds reasonably well through cooking, making dishes visually spectacular. Now grown commercially in California and Hawaii; available in health food stores and some major grocery chains.

common_name: Centennial Sweet Potato (LSU 1966 Orange Gulf Coast Standard)

planting.note: Plant slips after last frost when soil warms above 65°F; Centennial was released in 1966 by Louisiana State University Agricultural Center and was the dominant Southern sweet potato for 20+ years before Beauregard's introduction, noted for its excellent disease resistance, consistent yields, and reliable performance specifically in Gulf Coast growing conditions.

directions: Plant slips 12–18 inches apart in rows 3–4 feet apart in loose, well-drained soil enriched with compost; avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes excessive vining and reduces root yield. Centennial was developed and released in 1966 by the Louisiana State University Agricultural Center, named to commemorate the 100th anniversary of LSU's establishment, and rapidly became the standard commercial sweet potato in Louisiana and throughout the Gulf South; it held that position for nearly two decades before Beauregard displaced it commercially in the late 1980s. Centennial produces medium to large, elongated, smooth, red-orange-skinned roots with deep orange, moist, sweet flesh of excellent quality; it was specifically selected for resistance to Fusarium wilt and other soilborne diseases persistent in Gulf Coast sweet potato production. Now maintained by seed savers and specialty nurseries as an important historical variety of Louisiana and Gulf South agriculture; excellent for home garden use with low inputs.

common_name: White Jersey Sweet Potato (Dry-Type White Heirloom)

planting.note: Plant slips after last frost when soil warms above 65°F; White Jersey is an old American heirloom sweet potato grown in New Jersey since at least the mid-1800s, producing tan to buff-white-skinned, cream-to-white-fleshed roots of the 'dry' (starchy) type preferred in traditional American cooking before moist orange-fleshed types became dominant in the late 19th century.

directions: Plant slips 12–18 inches apart in well-prepared, loose, well-drained, sandy loam soil after soil warms above 65°F; White Jersey produces best in sandy or light soils. White Jersey is an American heirloom sweet potato representing the 'dry' type that was dominant in American cooking before the mid-20th century when moist orange-fleshed varieties became the standard; grown in New Jersey's sweet potato tradition since at least the 1850s, it produces pale buff to tan-skinned roots with cream-white flesh that cooks to a dry, fluffy, mealy texture resembling an Irish potato more than a modern sweet potato. The flavor is mild, subtle, and lightly sweet without the intense caramel sweetness of orange varieties. This dry, starchy texture was highly prized in traditional American cooking for sweet potato pie (which sets firmer), sweet potato biscuits, and baked goods where a drier texture improved the final product. Now maintained by seed savers as a historical artifact of early American sweet potato culture; an excellent educational variety for understanding the dramatic change in American sweet potato preferences over 150 years.
